coredata.dc:title: Capacitance effect of ZnIn2S4 with delicate morphology control on photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Dong
Zhang, Tingyuan
Wang, Jiajia
Wang, Wei
Lin, Keying
Ma, Baojun
Zhang, Fuxiang
coredata.dc:description: The capacitance effect of semiconductor photocatalysts for hydrogen production is significant; however, the intrinsic role of capacitance in relation to the morphologies of photocatalysts remains unclear. Herein, we designed three different morphologies of ZnIn2S4 semiconductor material—spherical, bulk, and rod-like—to determine their capacitance. In this study, the small capacitance characteristics of spherical ZnIn2S4 (0.363F/g) enabled photoelectrons to quickly transfer to the surface and participated in the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction, leading to a photocatalytic activity of 159 μmol/h/g. When TiVAlC nanoparticle with Mn+1AXn (MAX) nanostructure was employed as efficient co-catalyst, TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S achieved an increased activity of 285 μmol/h/g at 420 nm, which was 1.75 times that of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-B and 2.39 times that of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-R. This enhanced performance was attributed to the synergistic effect of the high-efficiency semiconductor photocatalyst ZnIn2S4-S with small capacitance and the superior conductivity of the Mxene material TiVAlC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic hydrogen production
Capacitive catalysis
ZnIn2S4
Morphology
TiVAlC

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies
Preparation of different TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4 composite catalysts
Structural characterization and photocatalytic activity of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies
Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical characterization of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies
Structure and photocatalytic activity characterization of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4
Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical characterization of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of ZnIn2S4; SEM images of ZnIn2S4: (b) ZnIn2S4-S, (c) ZnIn2S4-B, and (d) ZnIn2S4-R.

(a) Capacitance curve at 250 mV/s sweep speed, (b) Capacitance density curve, (c) Hydrogen production and hydrogen production per unit capacitance of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies, (d) Transient photocurrent responses, (e) Linear sweep voltammetry curve, (f) Electrochemical impedance spectra, (g) UV–VIS diffuse reflection curve, (h) Photoluminescence spectrm, and (i) Steady-state surface photovoltage spectrum of ZnIn2S4.
(a) XRD patterns of TiVAlC cocatalyst and ZnIn2S4 supported TiVAlC, (b) Different morphologies ZnIn2S4 and different ZnIn2S4 loaded with TiVAlC (20 % TiVAlC).
XPS of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S composite co-catalyst (a) Survey, (b) Zn 2p, (c) In 3d, (d) S 2p, (e) Ti 2p, (f) V 2p, (g) Al 2p, and (h) C1s.
(a–c) HRTEM of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S, (d) EDS-Mapping images of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S.

(a) Linear sweep voltammetry curve, (b) AC impedance spectrum, (c) Photocurrent response spectrum of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4, (d) UV–VIS diffuse reflectance curve, (e) Photoluminescence spectrum, and (f) Fluorescence lifetime curve of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4.
Photocatalytic H2 production mechanism of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4.

head.ce:title: Capacitance effect of ZnIn2S4 with delicate morphology control on photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Coal Utilization and Green Chemical Engineering
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Coal Utilization and Green Chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ningxia University, Yinchuan 750021, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Catalysis, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian National Laboratory for Clean Energy, The Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemistry for Energy Materials (iChEM), Zhongshan Road 457, Dalian 116023, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dalian National Laboratory for Clean Energy
The Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemistry for Energy Materials (iChEM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Catalysis, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian National Laboratory for Clean Energy, The Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemistry for Energy Materials (iChEM), Zhongshan Road 457, Dalian 116023, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The capacitance effect of semiconductor photocatalysts for hydrogen production is significant; however, the intrinsic role of capacitance in relation to the morphologies of photocatalysts remains unclear. Herein, we designed three different morphologies of ZnIn2S4 semiconductor material—spherical, bulk, and rod-like—to determine their capacitance. In this study, the small capacitance characteristics of spherical ZnIn2S4 (0.363F/g) enabled photoelectrons to quickly transfer to the surface and participated in the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction, leading to a photocatalytic activity of 159 μmol/h/g. When TiVAlC nanoparticle with Mn+1AXn (MAX) nanostructure was employed as efficient co-catalyst, TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S achieved an increased activity of 285 μmol/h/g at 420 nm, which was 1.75 times that of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-B and 2.39 times that of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-R. This enhanced performance was attributed to the synergistic effect of the high-efficiency semiconductor photocatalyst ZnIn2S4-S with small capacitance and the superior conductivity of the Mxene material TiVAlC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic water splitting offers a promising approach for clean, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly production of hydrogen by utilizing solar energy [1–4]. Semiconductor plays a pivotal role in photocatalysis, serving as the fundamental component responsible for solar light harvesting, charge separation and transportation, and the catalytic hydrogen evolution reactions [5–7]. However, photocatalysts formed from pristine semiconductor typically suffer from rapid charge recombination, limited light absorption, and poor stability, which leads to low photocatalytic activities [8]. Therefore, manipulating semiconductors presents a valuable strategy to overcome these limitations and enhance photocatalytic efficiency.

Morphology is a critical factor to determine the performance of semiconductor. Hence, manipulating the morphology of semiconductor is as important as the design of semiconductor materials [9–11]. Morphology control plays a critical role in optimizing the performance of semiconductor-based photocatalysts, as the shape and size of the material directly influence its surface area, active sites, and light absorption properties [12,13]. For instance, ZnO photocatalyst with flower-shaped, rod-like, belt-like, and sea urchin-shaped nanostructures can enhance photocatalytic performance remarkably [14]. He et al. [15] reported that the unique morphology of g-C3N4 enhanced electron transfer and charge separation, leading to a significant improvement in photocatalytic efficiency. Similarly, Wang et al. [16] investigated Ag2O photocatalysts in different morphology and found that cubic morphology exhibited the highest degradation rate. These studies confirmed that morphology control is an effective strategy for enhancing photocatalytic hydrogen production on semiconductors. However, the underlying mechanisms by which morphology influenced photocatalytic hydrogen production performance require further elucidation.

A well-controlled semiconductor morphology can increase surface area, optimize active site distribution, and enhance ion diffusion pathways, all of which improve charge storage capacity and the capacitive effect. [17]. Capacitance catalysis has become a research hotspot in the photocatalytic hydrogen production field. To date, researchers have explored numerous semiconductor materials to investigate the effect of capacitance effect in hydrogen evolution [18–20]. Recent work by Ma et al. has examined comprehensive and rigorous research on the capacitance effect in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The capacitances of non-noble metal such as Mo2C [21], the co-catalyst Zn-Ni5P4 [22], W-WC heterojunction [23], and composite co-catalyst Pt/Mo2N [24] exhibited strong capacitance catalysis in photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The co-catalyst with suitable specific capacitance can effectively store photo-excited electrons from semiconductor, thereby enhancing the overall photocatalysis performance. However, the relationship between the morphology and capacitance of the semiconductor itself in photocatalytic hydrogen production remains a challenge. It is worth noting that CdS semiconductor with smaller capacitance is adept at transporting photoelectrons generated by light excitation to the semiconductor surface, thus effectively reducing the recombination of photogenerated charge-holes [21]. Whether this correlation between smaller capacitance and enhanced semiconductor performance is a general rule still requires further verification.

ZnIn2S4 is one of the most prominent semiconductors for photocatalytic applications due to its suitable bandgap, good stability, and relatively facile synthesis [25–27]. However, as far as we know, the impact of morphology control of ZnIn2S4 on capacitance and photocatalytic hydrogen production have not been reported. Hence, we designed three different morphologies of ZnIn2S4 semiconductor—spherical, bulk, and rod-like—to determine their capacitance. The small capacitance characteristics of spherical ZnIn2S4 (0.363F/g) enabled the highest photocatalytic activity of 159 μmol/h/g. Moreover, we loaded the TiVAlC co-catalyst onto ZnIn2S4, further enhancing its photocatalytic hydrogen production performance due to its excellent electrical and thermal conductivity. This also highlighted the significant role of capacitive catalysis in photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Indium trichloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., while TiVAlC was obtained from Suzhou Beike Nanotechnology Co., Ltd. Additionally, all other chemicals were sourced from China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation. All reagents utilized in this study were of analytical grade and were directly employed in experiments without the need for further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies
ce:section.ce:para: The spherical ZnIn2S4 was prepared via a hydrothermal method. Specifically, 1 mmol of ZnCl2, 2 mmol of InCl3·4H2O, and 8 mmol of thioacetamide (TAA) were dissolved in 40 ml distilled water. Following 30 min of stirring, the mixture was transferred to a 100 ml Teflon liner and heated at 180°C for 18 h. Subsequently, the resulting product was washed with distilled water and ethanol to obtain the precipitate, which was then dried at 80°C for 12 h. Finally, the spherical ZnIn2S4 powder was obtained after grinding.

To obtain bulk ZnIn2S4, 0.3 mmol of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.6 mmol of In (NO3)3·4.5H2O were dissolved in 35 ml of deionized water and stirred until complete dissolution occurred. Subsequently, 5 ml of TAA (2.4 mmol) was added, and the mixture was continuously stirred for 2 h. The resulting solution was then transferred to a 100 ml Teflon liner autoclave and heated at 160°C for 6 h. Afterward, the precipitate was obtained by washing with distilled water and ethanol, followed by drying at 50°C for 48 h. Bulk ZnIn2S4 powder was obtained after grinding.

For the preparation of rod-like ZnIn2S4, 4.5 mg of In(NO3)3·4.5H2O and 5.5 mg of terephthalic acid (H2BDC) were dispersed in 2 ml DMF solution and heated at 120 °C for 30 min. The white precipitate of MIL-68 (In) was collected by centrifugation, washed three times with ethanol, and finally dried in a vacuum oven at 60°C. Then, 20 mg of the prepared MIL-68 (In) was dispersed in 30 ml of ethanol solution, followed by the addition of 750 μL of 0.1 M ZnCl2 solution and 200 mg thiourea. The resulting mixture was stirred until the thiourea was completely dissolved. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, tightly sealed, and heated at 180°C for 3 h. Finally, the product was washed three times with ethanol and dried in a vacuum oven at 60°C. The rod-like ZnIn2S4 powder was obtained after grinding. The spherical ZnIn2S4, bulk ZnIn2S4 and rod-like ZnIn2S4 were named as ZnIn2S4-S, ZnIn2S4-B and ZnIn2S4-R, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of different TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4 composite catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 g of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies was placed in 15 ml of ethanol using ultrasonication to achieve uniform dispersion. Subsequently, a certain amount of TiVAlC was added at a rate of 500 r/min, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature until completely dry. The resulting product was collected and thoroughly ground to obtain a uniform powder. The resulting powders were named TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S, TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-B, TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-R, respectively.

The phase of the catalyst was characterized by SA-HF3 (Japan) X-ray diffractometer. The morphology of the catalyst was observed using scanning electron microscope (SEM) (ZEISS Sigma 300, Germany). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of the sample were obtained via JEOL JEM-F200 (Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi) was used to obtain the information of the surface element type, element chemical state, and electron energy level distribution of the catalyst material. The UV/visible diffuse reflectance and photoluminescence spectra of the samples were obtained by UV-3600 spectrometer (Japan) and Edinburgh FLS 1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (UK), respectively. The monochromatic light excitation SPV signal was obtained by a 500 W xenon lamp (CHF-XM-500 W) through a grating monochromator (Omni-3700). The voltage formed by the built-in electric field was amplified by a lock-in amplifier (SR830) to determine the surface photovoltage signal of the required photocatalyst.

The photocatalytic activity was evaluated using LabSolor-H2 automatic trace gas on-line analysis system. 50 mg of sample and 100 ml of triethanolamine aqueous solution (10% triethanolamine) were placed into the reactor and uniformly mixed by ultrasonication. Prior to the reaction, the system was evacuated to a vacuum state, followed by irradiation with a 300 W xenon lamp filtered at 420 nm for 1h. Throughout the process, the system was maintained under 5°C circulating condensed water. The hydrogen produced during the reaction was detected by a GC700 gas chromatography (Shanghai Tianmei Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd.) with nitrogen serving as the carrier gas.

Electrochemical testing was conducted using a standard three-electrode system on the CH760D electrochemical workstation. In this setup, FTO conductive glass coated with the catalyst material served as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl was utilized as the counter electrode, and a graphite electrode was employed as the reference electrode. A 0.5 M saturated Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte. Various electrochemical tests, including capacitance density curve (CV), linear sweep voltammetry curve (LSV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and transient photocurrent test (TPR), were performed. The specific capacitance was calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
C = ∫ I d V / m · k · Δ E
where C (F/g) is specific capacitance, ∫IdV is integral area of the CV curve, m (mg) is the mass of sample coated on FTO, k (V/s) is the scan rate, and ΔE (V) is potential window. The transient photocurrent test of the catalyst material was carried out under the irradiation from a 300 W xenon lamp equipped with a 420 nm filter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization and photocatalytic activity of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns of ZnIn2S4 with varying morphologies were analyzed, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). All three forms of ZnIn2S4 align well with the reference pattern PDF#65-2023 for hexagonal ZnIn2S4 [28]. The main diffraction peaks observed at 21.6°, 28.9°, and 47.2° correspond to (006), (008) and (110) crystal planes of ZnIn2S4, respectively, confirming the successful synthesis of the catalyst. In Fig. 1(b), the morphology of ZnIn2S4 nano-spheres was depicted, consisting of ultrathin nanosheets. Fig. 1(c) displayed ZnIn2S4 nano-blocks, where the surface was decorated with small particles and covered by nanosheets. Fig. 1(d) illustrated ZnIn2S4 nano-rods, which exhibited a sparser distribution compared to the nanosheets observed on the nanospheres' surface.

The photocatalytic performance of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies was presented in Fig. 2 (a). Among the samples, ZnIn2S4-S (nano-spheres) demonstrated the highest hydrogen production rate of 159 μmol/h/g. In contrast, the photocatalytic water-splitting activities of ZnIn2S4-B (nano-blocks) and ZnIn2S4-R (nano-rods) were significantly lower, of 81 μmol/h/g and 71 μmol/h/g, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical characterization of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the electron transfer processes in the catalyst, photoelectrochemical tests were conducted using a 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. Fig. 2(b) shows the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies at a scan rate of 250 mV/s, with the measurement range of 0 to 0.1 V. The difference in current density for each catalyst at the same potential corresponds to the capacitance magnitude. As shown in Fig. 2(b), ZnIn2S4-S exhibited the smallest current density difference and, consequently, the lowest capacitance. In contrast, ZnIn2S4-B and ZnIn2S4-R exhibited significantly larger capacitance values compared to ZnIn2S4-S.

Fig. 2(c) illustrated the capacitance density curves for ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies, tested at scan rates of 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, and 0.30 V/s. Linear fitting was performed, with the obtained slope corresponding to the capacitance value. The capacitance values for ZnIn2S4-S, ZnIn2S4-B, and ZnIn2S4-R were 0.36F/g, 0.83F/g, and 0.92F/g, respectively. These results demonstrate that lower capacitance in ZnIn2S4 is associated with higher catalytic activity. Photocatalysts with lower capacitance are more efficient at facilitating the separation and transfer of photo-excited electrons, thereby enhancing hydrogen production on the photocatalyst surface. This is likely due to the weaker electron storage capacity of semiconductors with lower capacitance, which promotes faster electron transfer and, consequently, improves catalytic efficiency. The normalized photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies based on unit specific capacitance is illustrated in Fig. S1. It is evident that the normalized hydrogen production activity of ZnIn2S4-S is the highest, at 441.67 μmol/h/F. Following is bulk ZnIn2S4-B, and rod-shaped ZnIn2S4-R, with values of 97.59 μmol/h/F and 77.17 μmol/h/F, respectively. Intuitively, it can be analyzed that the semiconductor photocatalyst with smaller capacitance exhibits faster electron transport and possesses more advantages in photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Photocatalytic hydrogen production activity was also influenced by the sample's overpotential [29]. ZnIn2S4-S displayed the lowest hydrogen production overpotential, indicating that it faced the smallest energy barrier during the H2 reduction reaction, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency (Fig. 2(d)). Conversely, ZnIn2S4-R exhibited a higher hydrogen production overpotential, which delayed H2 reduction reaction dynamic reaction rate. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) provided insight into the charge transfer rates at the catalyst-electrode interface. The impedance spectra for ZnIn2S4-S, ZnIn2S4-B, and ZnIn2S4-R were depicted in Fig. 2(e). The Nyquist plot revealed that ZnIn2S4-S had the smallest semicircular radius, followed by ZnIn2S4-B and ZnIn2S4-R. A smaller Nyquist radius indicated lower interfacial charge transfer resistance, which facilitated more efficient and rapid electron transport [30,31]. Therefore, ZnIn2S4-S demonstrated the highest photocatalytic hydrogen production performance among all the samples tested.

The instantaneous photocurrent-time curves of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies measured under cyclic open-closed light irradiation with visible light, were shown in Fig. 2(f). ZnIn2S4-S exhibited the highest photocurrent response indicating the generation of a substantial number of photoexcited electrons upon light illumination, which correlated to its highest photocatalytic hydrogen production activity. ZnIn2S4-B demonstrated a moderate response, while ZnIn2S4-R showed the lowest photocurrent response, likely due to its larger capacitance. The strong electron storage capacity associated with the larger capacitance of ZnIn2S4-R impeded the rapid transfer of photogenerated electrons to the photocatalyst surface, resulting in the recombination of electrons and holes within the semiconductor structure [32,33].

To investigate the light-harvesting capabilities of ZnIn2S4 with different morphologies, UV–visible spectroscopy was conducted. As shown in Fig. 2(g), ZnIn2S4-S exhibited the red shift of absorption edge with broader visible light region, allowing it to capture more light and generate a greater number of photoexcited electrons for superior hydrogen production. In contrast, ZnIn2S4-B and ZnIn2S4-R showed a tiny red shift with wider band gaps compared to ZnIn2S4-S, which correlated with their lower photocatalytic performance.

The recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes can be characterized by steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Low PL intensity was indicative of efficient charge separation and superior photocatalytic hydrogen production performance [34,35]. As illustrated in Fig. 2(h), ZnIn2S4-R displayed the highest PL intensity, reflecting a high recombination rate of photoexcited electrons and holes, which restricted charge separation and migration. Conversely, ZnIn2S4-S exhibited the lowest PL intensity, signifying efficient electron-hole separation, enabling more photoexcited electrons to participate in the hydrogen reduction reaction. This result aligned with the concept that catalysts with smaller capacitance allow for faster electron transfer, minimizing carrier recombination.

Surface photovoltage (SPV) measured the photoinduced electron transition on solid surfaces and reflected the separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs [36,37]. Fig. 2(i) showed that ZnIn2S4-S had the highest SPV signal, indicating that upon exposure to high-energy monochromatic light, it generated a substantial number of photoexcited electrons and holes. These charge carriers diffused towards the space charge region of the semiconductor, where they were separated by the built-in electric field, creating a significant photovoltage and thus demonstrating effective charge separation. In contrast, ZnIn2S4-R showed the lowest SPV signal due to its higher capacitance, which impeded electron transfer and reduces the efficiency of electric field-driven charge separation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and photocatalytic activity characterization of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4
ce:section.ce:para: The MAX phase material, TiVAlC, was recognized for its excellent conductivity, making it a promising candidate co-catalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. Given the relatively low photocatalytic hydrogen production activity of ZnIn2S4 on its own, the incorporation of the TiVAlC co-catalyst into the three ZnIn2S4 morphologies significantly enhanced their photocatalytic performance. The XRD analysis of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4 composite catalysts with three distinct morphologies of TiVAlC, each containing 20% TiVAlC loading, was shown in Fig. 3 (a). The characteristic diffraction peak of TiVAlC, prominently observed at 40°, confirmed the successful integration of TiVAlC with ZnIn2S4. As depicted in Fig. S2(a), TiVAlC exhibited a layered nano-structure. SEM images of the composite catalysts with the three different morphologies after TiVAlC loading were provided in Fig. S2(b–d), where the layered TiVAlC was clearly seen on the surface of each composite catalyst, while the ZnIn2S4 photocatalyst retained its original morphology.

Various amounts of TiVAlC were successfully loaded onto ZnIn2S4-S (Fig. S3), with the optimal TiVAlC loading determined to be 20 wt%. This loading exhibited the highest photocatalytic hydrogen production, as shown in Fig. S4. The TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S composite catalyst, which had the highest hydrogen production activity among pure ZnIn2S4-S samples, achieved a hydrogen production rate of 285 μmol/h/g, 1.8 times higher than that of the unmodified ZnIn2S4-S. Additionally, the 20%TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S exhibited remarkable photocatalytic stability (Fig. S4(b)), with the original performance largely maintained after 20 h of cycling runs. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the hydrogen production activity of ZnIn2S4-B and ZnIn2S4-R loaded with TiVAlC increased by 2.0 and 1.6 times, respectively. This enhancement demonstrated that the TiVAlC deposited on ZnIn2S4 acts as an electron-trapping agent, facilitating the separation of photogenerated carriers and significantly improving the hydrogen production rate of the composite catalysts.

To analyze the bonding properties and chemical states of the elements in the 20 wt% TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S nanocomposites, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the XPS spectrum confirmed the presence of Zn, In, S, Ti, V, Al, and C in the TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S composite. In the Zn 2p region (Fig. 4(b)), two distinct peaks were observed at 1022.1 and 1045.1 eV, corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of ZnIn2S4, respectively [38]. Fig. 4(c) showed two prominent peaks at 445.0 and 452.5 eV, attributed to In 2p5/2 and In 2p3/2 of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S. In Fig. 4(d), the peaks at 161.7 and 162.9 eV corresponded to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 [39]. The Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 4(e)) revealed dual signals of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 at 458.4 eV and 464.1 eV, respectively [40]. In Fig. 4(f), the peaks at 523.9 eV and 516.7 eV were attributed to V 2p1/2 and V 2p3/2, respectively [23]. The peak at 74.7 eV in Fig. 4(g) corresponded to Al3+ 2p of Al2O3 [41], while the peak at 77.5 eV was assigned to the Al-S bond formed by the combination of Al and ZnIn2S4 in the lattice [42]. The XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 4(h)) showed characteristic binding energy peaks at 288.5 eV, 284.7 eV, 286.8 eV, and 285.6 eV, corresponding to the CO bond, sp2 hybridized CC bond, CO bond, and sp3 hybridized CC bond, respectively [22].

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S were presented in Fig. 5 (a–c). The calculated d-spacing values of 0.204 nm and 0.223 nm corresponded to the (511) plane of ZnIn2S4 and the (103) plane of TiVAlC, respectively. Field-emission HRTEM energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (FE-HRTEM EDAX) spectra of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S (Fig. S5) revealed atomic fractions of Zn, In, and S as 10.3: 25.51: 41.71, respectively, closely aligning with the stoichiometric ratio of ZnIn2S4 (1: 2: 4). Additionally, the presence of substrate C was consistent with the 1: 1: 1: 1 stoichiometric ratio of TiVAlC, and no impurity elements were detected. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis corroborated the findings from XRD characterization [43]. The element distribution map (Fig. 5d) showed a highly dispersed distribution of elements in the selected area, indicating strong interaction between TiVAlC and ZnIn2S4, which promoted effective charge separation and mitigates charge recombination during photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical characterization of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the hydrogen production overpotentials of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S, TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-B, and TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-R significantly decreased after the incorporation of TiVAlC compared with Fig. 2(d). This reduction demonstrates that TiVAlC facilitated proton reduction catalysis, lowering the hydrogen production overpotential of ZnIn2S4 and enhancing the likelihood of photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen production. Additionally, Fig. 6(b) revealed that the interface transfer resistance in the TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S system was the lowsest after loading the TiVAlC cocatalyst. The Nyquist plot illustrated that the TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S system exhibited the smallest semicircle radius, indicating the lowest interface electron transfer resistance. This reduction in resistance suggested that more photogenerated electrons in the composite system can effectively participate in the hydrogen production reaction, thereby significantly enhancing the photocatalytic performance [44]. Moreover, all of the composite catalyst system, constructed by incorporating TiVAlC, markedly improved the electron transfer efficiency of the semiconductor catalyst under photoexcitation (Fig. 6(c)). TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S exhibited the highest photocurrent response signal, signifying that TiVAlC can rapidly capture photogenerated electrons from ZnIn2S4-S, resulting in excellent charge transfer capabilities and superior photocatalytic performance [45].

UV–visible diffuse reflectance tests were performed on the three TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4 composites [46], as shown in Fig. 6(d). Among the composites, TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S exhibited the broadest absorption edge, followed by TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-B and TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-R. Correspondingly, the band gaps of the materials followed the order: TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-R > TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-B > TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S. A narrower band gap promoted the transition of photogenerated electrons, allowing more electrons to participate in the proton reduction reaction, consistent with the observed photocatalytic hydrogen production activity.

The steady-state PL spectrum in Fig. 6(e) showed that ZnIn2S4-S had a weak PL signal, indicating effective interfacial charge transfer and suppression of photogenerated charge recombination. In contrast, the pronounced PL quenching observed for ZnIn2S4-R suggested a high recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes, resulting in fewer electrons being available for hydrogen proton reduction.

The fluorescence lifetime analysis further investigated the behavior of photogenerated carriers. The emission decay curve was fitted using a double-exponential kinetic model, yielding two decay components. The average fluorescence lifetime reflected the overall emission decay behavior. As shown in Fig. 6(f), TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S demonstrated an average fluorescence lifetime of 1.26 ns, TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-B exhibited 1.40 ns, and TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-R had the longest lifetime of 1.55 ns. The shorter fluorescence lifetime of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S indicated enhanced electron transfer efficiency, facilitated by non-radiative energy transfer processes, thereby improving the photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency [21,47]. Fig. 7 illustrates the photocatalytic hydrogen production mechanism of TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4. Upon absorption of light energy by ZnIn2S4, electron-hole pairs are generated at the top of the valence band. The electrons are then transferred to the bottom of the conduction band, while the holes remain at the top of the valence band. The holes react with triethanolamine, and the electrons at the bottom of the conduction band are captured by TiVAlC, facilitating the catalysis of protons to produce hydrogen.

In this study, we investigated three distinct morphologies of ZnIn2S4 namely, ZnIn2S4-S (nano-spheres), ZnIn2S4-B (nano-blocks), and ZnIn2S4-R (nano-rods). ZnIn2S4-S exhibited the highest normalized hydrogen production activity of 441.67 μmol/h/F, followed by ZnIn2S4-B and ZnIn2S4-R, with values of 97.59 μmol/h/F and 77.17 μmol/h/F, respectively. Capacitance curve measurements revealed a strong correlation between the small specific capacitance of spherical ZnIn2S4-S and its superior hydrogen production activity. Moreover, the incorporation of TiVAlC resulted in further enhancement of photocatalytic hydrogen production performance, especially for TiVAlC/ZnIn2S4-S. The findings further demonstrate that smaller capacitance is associated with better semiconductor performance. Additionally, the co-catalyst loaded onto the semiconductor with the smallest capacitance exhibited the highest performance in photocatalytic H2 evolution.

Dong Feng: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Tingyuan Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jiajia Wang: Visualization, Software. Wei Wang: Visualization, Software. Keying Lin: Visualization, Software. Baojun Ma: Writing – review & editing. Fuxiang Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22462027), Talent of Ningxia (2021GKLRLX07), and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningxia (2021AAC03111, 2022AAC02015).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2024.131283.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
